title: Share of people who have depressive disorders, by age
subtitle: Estimated share of people who have  [depressive disorders](#dod:depressive_disorders), whether or not they are diagnosed, based on representative surveys, medical data and statistical modelling.
caption:
Share of people who have depressive disorders, by age, World, 2023
Estimated share of people who have depressive disorders, whether or not they are diagnosed, based on representative surveys, medical data and statistical modelling.

This horizontal bar chart displays the estimated share of the global population with depressive disorders across fifteen distinct age categories for the year 2023. The data, which is age-standardized to allow for comparisons between groups, measures the percentage of people affected regardless of whether they have received a formal diagnosis. The values range from a low of 0.98% to a high of 5.46%. The distribution shows a distinct pattern where prevalence remains low in childhood, rises significantly during adolescence, and stabilizes at a higher level throughout adulthood and old age. The category for Ages 5-14 records the lowest share at 0.98%. A substantial increase occurs in the Ages 15-19 group, which stands at 4.55%. The share continues to climb through young adulthood, with Ages 20-24 at 4.78% and Ages 25-29 at 4.91%. The highest prevalence rates appear in the older age brackets. The Ages 65-69 group shows the maximum value on the chart at 5.46%, followed closely by Ages 60-64 at 5.4% and Ages 70+ at 5.36%. The middle-age groups, such as Ages 40-44 and Ages 45-49, hold steady values around 5.28% and 5.29% respectively. The aggregate figure for All ages sits at 4%, while the specific Age-standardized metric is recorded at 3.81%. The visual arrangement highlights a clear gradient where the share of people with depressive disorders increases with age from childhood through to the late sixties before leveling off.
